episode_meta.title: Ivar Krustok: How Estonia's Media Giant Builds AI That Actually Works

ai_tools: ChatGPT / OpenAI (editorial licenses, prompting, personal use)
Microsoft Azure (cloud infrastructure and AI model hosting)
Microsoft Fabric (cloud analytics platform for data infrastructure)
Microsoft Copilot (integrated into Power BI dashboards)
Hugging Face (open-source model repository and hosting)
Manus AI (agentic AI toolkit for research and task automation)
Vercel V0.dev (AI-powered prototyping and code generation)
Google Stitch / Galileo (AI Figma design generation)
Breach Media (third-party summarization and polling vendor)
Amazon AWS (hosting for image generation campaign)
Vector database (for archival RAG tool, unspecified vendor)
Chinese deep research model (unnamed, self-hosted capability)

use_cases.description: Transcription tools for Estonian language content, enabling accurate speech-to-text where previously the small language was poorly supported
use_cases.outcome: Estonian language transcription quality improved significantly with generative AI; barrier for small languages narrowed considerably

use_cases.description: Summarization tools for editorial staff to quickly process content
Live cross-Baltic translation feed: real-time translation of news content across Estonian, Latvian, Lithuanian, and Russian, enabling editors to read all three countries' news in their local language
use_cases.outcome: Editors can now discover stories from other Baltic states in real time; dramatically reduced time to share information across countries; cost dropped to near-zero due to falling token prices

use_cases.description: Internal ChatGPT-like platform: in-house chat interface built on Azure-hosted and Hugging Face open-source models, mixing and matching specialized models to avoid multiple vendor subscriptions for 400-500+ employees
use_cases.outcome: Provides secure AI access without data leaving company infrastructure; avoids cost of 400+ individual ChatGPT licenses; not yet fully optimized for user expectations

use_cases.description: Archival RAG tool: 25 years of multilingual content indexed in a vector database, enabling journalists to query historical coverage, find quotes from specific people over specific periods
use_cases.outcome: All text from 25 years across three to four languages searchable via natural language; users must still be more specific than with ChatGPT to get good results

use_cases.description: Court case monitoring tool: automated ingestion and summarization of new court cases from open governmental data, pushed as Teams notifications to editorial staff
use_cases.tool_used: LLM summarization, Microsoft Teams push notifications
use_cases.outcome: Approximately 5-6 articles published in first month that would otherwise have been missed; saves journalist time; evolved from intended chat interface to push notification model

use_cases.description: Open governmental data integration: connecting court cases, tax returns, procurement databases, and other public documents for investigative journalism
use_cases.outcome: Makes impossible-to-manually-process volumes of public documents accessible to editorial staff

use_cases.description: Copilot-powered dashboard querying: editors can ask natural language questions of Power BI dashboards connected to additional data tables (e.g., advertising performance, anomalies, top clients)
use_cases.outcome: Replaces manual filtering across multiple tabs; launched for heavy users with direct Power BI access

use_cases.description: Microsoft Teams ad inventory bot: agentic bot connected directly to data (not dashboards) that answers questions about advertising inventory, placements, and sales figures
use_cases.outcome: Launched in test to limited users; required significant training on industry-specific terminology; took longer than expected

use_cases.description: Planned daily editorial performance summaries: morning push notifications summarizing previous day's article performance, page views, subscriptions, with follow-up recommendations
use_cases.outcome: Envisioned as push-based data delivery to editorial staff who should not need to consult dashboards; not yet deployed

use_cases.description: Audience-facing AI image generation campaign: users upload selfies and receive AI-generated images of themselves wearing traditional Estonian national dance costumes
use_cases.tool_used: Hugging Face open-source image model, hosted on Amazon AWS
use_cases.outcome: Approximately 20,000 images generated; high engagement; no user images stored or used for training; challenge with Estonian facial expressions not matching model's smiling defaults

use_cases.description: Third-party summarization and polling on audience-facing articles
use_cases.outcome: Outsourced to third party to optimize cost and workload; evaluating whether AI features from vendor are worth the premium

use_cases.description: Rapid tool prototyping workflow: using Manus AI for API documentation research, Google Stitch for Figma design generation, and Vercel V0.dev for code generation with connected API and design, producing working prototypes in hours
use_cases.tool_used: Manus AI, Google Stitch (Galileo), Vercel V0.dev, ChatGPT
use_cases.organization: Delfi Media (personal workflow of Ivar Krustok)
use_cases.outcome: Google Trends prototype built in 3 hours with live data; enables showing working prototypes to business stakeholders instead of slide decks

use_cases.description: Agent-based web scraping of governmental databases: using AI agents that operate normal browsers to gather formatted data from public databases not designed for automated access
use_cases.outcome: Agents behave like human browsers, can run overnight, gather data impossible for humans to scrape at scale; planned focus for second half of year

use_cases.description: AI literacy and upskilling program: biweekly open discussions with editorial staff, shifting from top-down training to peer-driven idea sharing and identifying scalable use cases
use_cases.outcome: Initial top-down training approach deemed insufficient; pivoted to identifying specific high-ROI use cases from power users before broader rollout

key_claims.claim: Estonia has the highest digital-only subscriber penetration rate in the world at around 21% of the population
key_claims.speaker: Ivar Krustok

key_claims.claim: Estonian, as a Finno-Ugric language on a completely separate language tree from most other languages, saw a disproportionately large improvement in AI language tool quality compared to other languages with the generative AI boom
key_claims.speaker: Ivar Krustok

key_claims.claim: The price of AI tokens has dropped to 'almost nothing' over the past one to two years, making previously expensive tools like cross-language translation economically viable at scale
key_claims.speaker: Ivar Krustok

key_claims.claim: AI-generated Estonian text has a detectable quality called 'Est English' where grammar is correct but sentence structure follows English patterns rather than native Estonian patterns
key_claims.speaker: Ivar Krustok

key_claims.claim: The court case monitoring tool produced 5-6 articles in its first month that would otherwise have gone unnoticed by the editorial team
key_claims.speaker: Ivar Krustok

key_claims.claim: Audience-facing AI chat/search tools are not yet profitable for publishers - the monetization of a search box cannot match the ad revenue of multiple page views
key_claims.speaker: Ivar Krustok
key_claims.evidence_type: anecdotal - based on conversations with multiple publishers

key_claims.claim: Estonia's high direct traffic (driven by high trust in news) provides insulation from platform dependency on Google and Facebook algorithm changes
key_claims.speaker: Ivar Krustok

key_claims.claim: Building a solid data foundation took years but made migration to new platforms (Azure Synapse to Microsoft Fabric) relatively easy compared to organizations without clean foundations
key_claims.speaker: Ivar Krustok

key_claims.claim: A working Google Trends prototype with live data and custom design can be built from scratch in about 3 hours using AI-assisted development tools (Manus AI, Stitch, V0.dev)
key_claims.speaker: Ivar Krustok

key_claims.claim: Many vendors are adding AI features to existing products that may not be needed, increasing costs without proportional value to publishers
key_claims.speaker: Ivar Krustok
key_claims.evidence_type: opinion based on vendor evaluation experience

challenges.description: Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian are small languages with limited AI training data, resulting in lower model quality compared to English and other large languages
AI-generated Estonian text follows English sentence structure ('Est English') rather than native Estonian patterns, making AI-written content detectable and unnatural
Internal ChatGPT-like archival tool requires more specific prompting than commercial ChatGPT, creating a UX gap where users accustomed to ChatGPT's flexibility are frustrated
Industry-specific terminology (ad placements, inventory keywords) required extensive training for the Teams bot, taking much longer than anticipated
Token costs were initially prohibitive for running archival search across 25 years of multilingual content; team had to wait for pricing to come down before scaling
Audience-facing AI features (chat search, summarization) are not yet profitable: replacing page views with chat responses reduces ad revenue without clear monetization path
As a small-market publisher, Delfi cannot negotiate the same enterprise deals and free licenses that large English-language publishers receive from AI companies
AI image generation models default to smiling faces, which clashed with typical Estonian/Nordic facial expressions in user-uploaded photos, producing awkward results
National costume patterns are region-specific in Estonia; AI image generation had to be carefully constrained to avoid generating incorrect cultural patterns

AI literacy training was initially unsatisfying: providing ChatGPT licenses and general training did not translate to sustained adoption; needed to pivot to identifying specific high-value use cases first
Vendors adding AI features to existing products and charging premiums, when the non-AI features were already sufficient, creates cost pressure without proportional value
Building a proper data foundation took multiple years before any dashboards could be created; requires organizational patience and management support for long runway
Governmental databases are not designed for automated access, making traditional web scraping difficult; requires agent-based browser automation as a workaround

quotes.text: We have in Estonia the highest digital only subscriber penetration rate in the world. So we have around 21% people who are digital subscribers and they're paying for the content every month.
quotes.speaker: Ivar Krustok
quotes.pedagogical_value: Establishes that even a very small country (1.3 million people) can achieve world-leading digital subscription penetration, challenging the assumption that market size determines digital success

quotes.text: The sentences are built in a way that American language is built rather than how the Estonian language is built. So you yes, you understand exactly what it's trying to convey to you. But the forming of the language is incorrect.
quotes.speaker: Ivar Krustok
quotes.topic: AI-generated text quality in small languages
quotes.pedagogical_value: Illustrates a specific and underreported problem with LLMs for small languages: structural anglicization rather than outright errors, useful for understanding non-English AI deployment

quotes.text: Rather than maybe it becoming a chat interface, it's more of a Teams push notification with a quick summarization because you can see much more value being derived from this.
quotes.speaker: Ivar Krustok
quotes.topic: Evolving AI tool design based on real usage
quotes.pedagogical_value: Demonstrates how initial assumptions about AI tool formats (chat) often pivot once real user behavior reveals that push-based delivery provides more value than pull-based interfaces

quotes.text: It's easy to get a dashboard up and running but it's really hard and it's really testing intense to actually play a groundwork where okay, I want to add something and then it becomes easy because you have a really good foundation.
quotes.speaker: Ivar Krustok
quotes.pedagogical_value: Key insight about the hidden cost of skipping foundational data work; quick dashboards create technical debt while solid foundations enable agility later

quotes.text: I can already send them a test link. Like, okay, we talked about it a while ago that something like this would help. Like is this something you're envisioning and it would already have some sort of mock data already, live data from the API.
quotes.speaker: Ivar Krustok
quotes.topic: AI-powered rapid prototyping for stakeholder alignment
quotes.pedagogical_value: Shows how AI-assisted development tools collapse the gap between idea and working prototype, replacing months of discussion with immediate hands-on evaluation

quotes.text: I think this is the first sort of the revolution where I see that news publishers are more openly sharing what they're working on. So I think this is something that I was hoping that was years and years already it would be happening.
quotes.speaker: Ivar Krustok
quotes.pedagogical_value: Highlights a cultural shift in the news industry where AI challenges have fostered more open knowledge-sharing between competitors than previous technology waves

recommendations.advice: Organize AI strategy into three buckets: everyday editorial tools (transcription, translation, summarization), experimental long-term projects (internal ChatGPT, archival tools), and AI literacy/upskilling programs
recommendations.speaker: Ivar Krustok

recommendations.advice: For everyday editorial tools, prioritize speed of delivery over perfection: get basic tools ready quickly to provide value rather than spending months on UX polish and fine-tuning
recommendations.speaker: Ivar Krustok

recommendations.advice: Build your own AI platform (internal ChatGPT) when on a small market where you cannot negotiate enterprise-level deals; use open-source models from Hugging Face hosted on your own infrastructure to control costs and data privacy
recommendations.speaker: Ivar Krustok

recommendations.advice: Use third-party vendors for audience-facing AI features where cost optimization at scale is critical, but negotiate to strip out unnecessary AI add-ons that inflate pricing without proportional value
recommendations.speaker: Ivar Krustok

recommendations.advice: Invest in foundational data infrastructure before building AI tools on top; ensure the data layer is clean and well-structured even if it takes years, because migrations and new capabilities become easy with a solid foundation
recommendations.speaker: Ivar Krustok
recommendations.target_audience: Publishers starting data and AI initiatives

recommendations.advice: Use AI-assisted prototyping tools (Manus AI for API docs, Stitch/Galileo for design, V0.dev for code) to build working prototypes in hours instead of presenting slide decks, enabling faster stakeholder alignment and stress-testing of ideas
recommendations.speaker: Ivar Krustok
recommendations.target_audience: Innovation officers and product teams in media

recommendations.advice: Shift AI literacy training from top-down generic instruction to peer-driven discussions where power users share specific use cases that can be scaled across the organization
recommendations.speaker: Ivar Krustok
recommendations.target_audience: Media organizations running AI training programs

recommendations.advice: Be transparent with audiences about what happens to their data when using AI-powered features; this builds trust and differentiates responsible publishers from AI-generated content farms
recommendations.speaker: Ivar Krustok

recommendations.advice: Openly share AI learnings, failures, and use cases with other publishers; the value from collaborative knowledge-sharing far outweighs competitive concerns
recommendations.speaker: Ivar Krustok
